summary: I was born in Hanson, Oklahoma, on November 1, 1940. It's a small rural community just outside of Sallisaw. Although I moved away at an early age, I still have many family members there and remember it as my birthplace.

Hanson, where I was born, is just outside of Sallisaw, Oklahoma. While I didn’t grow up there, Sallisaw is part of the region connected to my early life and family roots. It was the nearest recognizable town to my birthplace.

My father served in World War II, and after the war ended, our family moved around quite a bit. That period shaped much of our early life as we relocated from Oklahoma to northern California, then Kansas, and finally to the Tulsa area. His service was a defining part of our family history.

After World War II, my family moved to Marysville, California, which was the first place we lived outside of Oklahoma. I started my elementary education there, attending school through the second grade before we moved again. It was an important early chapter in my childhood.

Following our time in California, we moved to Wichita, Kansas, where I finished fifth grade. It was another stop in our family's journey before settling in the Tulsa area. I have memories of school and life there during those formative years.

Eventually, my family settled in the Tulsa area, where my father worked as a principal for Catoosa Schools. From fifth grade onward, I attended schools in this region, graduating from Catoosa High School nearby. Tulsa became the base for much of my education and early career.

My father was a principal at Catoosa Schools when we moved to the Tulsa area. I attended Catoosa Schools from fifth grade through high school, which gave me a solid educational foundation. It was a close-knit community and an important part of my upbringing.

I graduated from Catoosa High School after attending Catoosa Schools from fifth grade on. It was the place where I completed my secondary education before moving on to college. The school was part of my formative years in the Tulsa area.

After high school, I attended Northeastern Oklahoma A&M College in Miami, Oklahoma, for two years. It was my first experience in higher education and helped prepare me for further studies. Miami was a step on my educational path before transferring to the University of Colorado.

Miami, Oklahoma, is where Northeastern Oklahoma A&M College is located, and I spent two years there after graduating from high school. It was an important place in my educational journey before I moved on to the University of Colorado. Miami marked my transition from local schooling to broader academic pursuits.

I transferred to the University of Colorado after two years at NEO A&M. I really loved Boulder and wanted to stay there, but I ended up taking a job with the Chapman Oil Company in Tulsa. My connection to the university also helped me get the internship through George Carlson, who was involved with their athletic program.

I did some graduate studies at the University of Tulsa after completing my undergraduate degree. It was part of my continued education in the Tulsa area, where I was also starting my career. The university played a role in my academic and professional development.

I worked a summer job for the Chapman Oil Company while I was still in school, which turned into a full-time career after graduation. The company put me through a training program, and I got to know the Chapmans personally, especially H.A. Chapman. It was through this company that I learned the oil business from the ground up.

George Carlson was the man who ran the Chapman business when I first got involved. He was a Rhodes Scholar and had been a football player and boxer at the University of Colorado, which helped me get my internship. He was very supportive and even took me to Southern Hills Country Club for brunch once, trying to encourage me to attend Colorado.

I remember going to Sunday brunch at Southern Hills Country Club with George Carlson early on. I had never heard of the place before, but I was very impressed by it. It was part of my introduction to the world of the Chapmans and Tulsa’s social scene.

Harry Allen Chapman, or H.A. Chapman as he was known, was the son of James A. Chapman and my employer. Born in 1919 and passing away in 1979 at age 59, he was a businessman who started Chapman Exploration Company. I worked closely with him, became a close friend, and accompanied him on trout fishing trips and car races. He was introverted but enjoyed my company, and I consider myself one of his confidantes.

James A. Chapman was H.A. Chapman's father and a quiet, gentlemanly man whom I met a few times. He was always well-dressed and inconspicuous despite his wealth. I had a memorable meeting with him early in my career when his attorney, John Rogers, arranged for me to visit his office. He seemed interested in knowing who was working with his son and gave me his blessing.

John Rogers was the attorney and confidante for James A. Chapman, and he played a key role in connecting me with the Chapmans. He was also instrumental in starting the University of Tulsa College of Law as its first dean. John Rogers arranged for me to meet James Chapman, which was an important moment early in my career.
cues: University of Tulsa College of Law founder

entity: University of Tulsa College of Law
summary: John Rogers, who was the attorney for James Chapman, was instrumental in starting the University of Tulsa College of Law and served as its first dean. While I did not attend law school there, this connection through Rogers linked me indirectly to the institution. It was part of the broader Tulsa educational and professional community I was involved with.

H.A. Chapman attended Cascia Hall Preparatory School in Tulsa, though I don't know much about his earlier education. It was part of his background before he became a businessman and started Chapman Exploration. Cascia Hall is a well-known school in Tulsa.

In the last few years of his life, H.A. Chapman was based out of Monte Carlo during the summers. He loved yachting and spent a lot of time on his yacht there. This European connection made my life easier since I traveled back and forth a lot.

The Chapman Special was a sports car that H.A. Chapman raced on the international circuit, including the 500. I went to several races with him and saw his passion for automobile racing firsthand. It was one of his youthful interests before he settled into the oil business.

My first assignment after joining Chapman Exploration full-time in 1963 was in Great Bend, Kansas. We had drilling rigs and an engineering office there, and I worked on the rigs with the roustabouts. It was a tough job in a flat, hot, and windy place, and I didn’t enjoy it much, but it was part of learning the oil business from the ground up.
